title: Gambas al Ajillo
subtitle: Spanish garlic prawns with olive oil and chilli
excerpt: Gambas al ajillo are prawns cooked in a sizzling bath of olive oil, garlic, and dried chilli. They cook in about five minutes and are eaten straight from the pan with bread to mop the oil. A classic Spanish tapa.
sourceNotes: Gambas al ajillo is one of the most ubiquitous tapas in Spain. The dish appears on every tapas menu and is made at home frequently. The essential elements are raw prawns, a generous amount of garlic, olive oil, and a little dried chilli.

content.text: Gambas al ajillo are prawns cooked in hot olive oil with a large amount of garlic and a little dried chilli. The oil becomes the sauce; it picks up the flavour of the garlic and the seafood and is as important as the prawns themselves. Bread to mop it up is not optional.

items.prepNote: large raw prawns, shelled and deveined
whole dried chilli, or 0.5 tsp chilli flakes

content.text: Pat the prawns dry with kitchen paper. Season with salt.

Heat {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} in a large frying pan over medium heat. Add the sliced garlic and whole dried chilli. Cook gently for 2 minutes until the garlic is very lightly golden but not brown. The oil should sizzle gently; if it's smoking, reduce the heat.

Increase the heat to medium-high. Add the prawns in a single layer. Cook for 1 minute, then flip and cook for 1 minute more. Add the wine if using and let it bubble for 30 seconds. The prawns are done when they are pink and opaque throughout; don't overcook them.
Remove from the heat. Scatter with parsley and serve immediately in the pan, with bread alongside and lemon wedges.

items.symptom: The garlic burns before the prawns are cooked
items.cause: Oil too hot in the infusing stage
items.fix: Infuse the garlic in the oil over medium to medium-low heat so it colours gently. Increase the heat only when the prawns go in.
Large prawns need about 1 minute each side over high heat. They are done the moment they turn from translucent to opaque. Remove from the heat immediately.

content.text: Gambas al ajillo is found on every tapas menu in Spain and is one of the dishes that most people try first at a tapas bar. The dish is at its best when the oil is generous and the garlic is just starting to colour. Serve it in a shallow clay dish (cazuela) if you have one; it stays hot longer and looks right.
